inference_steps: [53.474s to 79.495s] Chronologically, the first relevant exchange is at 53.474s-79.495s. It directly shows that the blonde sent a message and that the brunette came because of it: the blonde asks whether she got the message, the brunette says yes, and the blonde says she could not have guessed the brunette would be exactly there at 11. This grounds that the text arranged or caused the meeting.

[81.352s to 110.883s] The next exchange, at 81.352s-110.883s, shows what kind of meeting it was: a cliffside suicide/jump confrontation. In that exchange, the brunette asks why the blonde is there, and the blonde answers that she is there "to watch you jump," with nearby lines about dying, jumping, and getting married in peace. This grounds the setting and immediate purpose of the confrontation after the text-arranged meeting.

[115.777s to 143.468s] Only later, at 115.777s-143.468s, does the harsh rebuke become explicit: the blonde says the brunette has been bothering her for months and trying to ruin her relationship. Because these rebuking lines occur after the brunette has already arrived, they support the broader conflict but are weaker evidence that the original text was sent for the purpose of rebuking her.

[53.474s to 79.495s; 81.352s to 110.883s; 115.777s to 143.468s] The evidence does not explicitly say, in so many words, that the text was sent 'to lure the brunette to the cliffs.' What it does directly support is: the text brought the brunette to a specific place/time, and that meeting is then shown to be at the cliffs in a jump/suicide context. Among the answer choices, this makes D the closest supported mapping. Option B is weaker because the explicit rebuke is grounded later during the confrontation rather than as the stated purpose of the text itself. The other options are not supported by the transcript.

evidence.evidence_text: The transcript establishes that the blonde sent a message and that the brunette came to a specific place/time because of it. The blonde asks, "Did you get my message?" / "T'as reçu mon message ?" The brunette confirms receipt, and the blonde adds that she is not telepathic and could not have guessed the brunette would be "exactly here at 11h" / "pile poil ici à 11h."

After that message-arranged arrival, the confrontation is clearly in a suicide/jump setting at the cliffs. In the 81.352s-110.883s exchange, the brunette asks why the blonde is there, and the blonde answers, "To watch you jump" / "pour te regarder sauter." Nearby lines include "You want to see me die," "Go ahead jump," and "Let me get married in peace."

A later argument frames the broader conflict as romantic/relationship conflict. The blonde says the brunette has been bothering her for months and trying to mess up her relationship: "You've been annoying me for months" / "ça fait des mois que tu me fais chier" and "It's much more classy to try to screw up my couple!" The brunette counters with lines such as "Your shit couple that you don't even love your guy!" and "You don't love him, it's me you love!" followed by "I don't love you!" and "I left Pierre anyway."

answer: D. To lure the brunette to the cliffs.
